choice_context: A person is deciding which wine to buy or drink based on the description of the wine.
reasoning: In the wine domain, people often disagree on the balance between fruitiness and earthiness, the level of sweetness versus dryness, and the preference for bold versus subtle flavors. These preferences are shaped by cultural background, personal taste, and occasion (e.g., casual vs. formal). Fruit-forward wines appeal to those who enjoy ripe, sweet aromas, while earthy wines attract those who prefer complexity and terroir-driven profiles. Sweetness is a major axis of disagreement, with some preferring off-dry or dessert wines and others favoring dry, austere styles. Bold vs. subtle is another key axis, with bold wines often favored by those who enjoy high alcohol and intense tannins, and subtle wines preferred by those who appreciate nuance and balance.

dimensions.name: Fruity vs. Earthy

low_pole.label: Earthy
low_pole.description: Wines with notes of soil, mushrooms, leather, or forest floor; often aged in oak or with extended skin contact.
low_pole.typical_person: Wine connoisseurs or those who enjoy Old World styles like Burgundy or Barolo.

high_pole.label: Fruity
high_pole.description: Wines with pronounced fruit aromas and flavors, such as berries, citrus, or tropical fruits; often young and fresh.
high_pole.typical_person: Casual drinkers or those who prefer New World styles like California Zinfandel or Chilean Carmenère.

example_contrast.low_option: A 2015 Barolo Riserva with notes of truffle and dried herbs.
example_contrast.high_option: A 2022 New Zealand Sauvignon Blanc bursting with passionfruit and gooseberry.

dimensions.scoring_guidance: Earthy wines emphasize soil and age-related characteristics, while fruity wines highlight fresh, vibrant fruit flavors. A 1 would be deeply earthy, a 4 would be balanced between the two, and a 7 would be intensely fruity.

dimensions.name: Sweet vs. Dry

low_pole.label: Dry
low_pole.description: Wines with little to no residual sugar; crisp and refreshing, often with a clean finish.
low_pole.typical_person: Those who prefer crisp whites or tannic reds, or who drink wine with savory foods.

high_pole.label: Sweet
high_pole.description: Wines with noticeable residual sugar; often described as luscious, honeyed, or dessert-style.
high_pole.typical_person: Those who enjoy dessert wines, or who drink wine with sweet or spicy foods.

example_contrast.low_option: A 2021 Sancerre with a mineral-driven, dry profile.
example_contrast.high_option: A 2018 Sauternes with honeyed apricot and botrytis notes.

dimensions.scoring_guidance: Dry wines have little to no perceptible sweetness, while sweet wines are clearly sugary. A 1 would be bone dry, a 4 would be off-dry or balanced, and a 7 would be intensely sweet.

dimensions.name: Bold vs. Subtle

low_pole.label: Subtle
low_pole.description: Wines with restrained flavors, low alcohol, and delicate aromas; often described as elegant or refined.
low_pole.typical_person: Those who prefer Old World wines or enjoy food pairing with nuanced wines.

high_pole.label: Bold
high_pole.description: Wines with intense flavors, high alcohol, and pronounced tannins or oak; often described as powerful or full-bodied.
high_pole.typical_person: Those who enjoy New World reds or drink wine as a standalone experience.

example_contrast.low_option: A 2020 Chablis with crisp acidity and restrained stone fruit.
example_contrast.high_option: A 2016 Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon with bold blackberry and vanilla.

dimensions.scoring_guidance: Subtle wines are delicate and refined, while bold wines are intense and full-bodied. A 1 would be very light and delicate, a 4 would be balanced and medium-bodied, and a 7 would be rich and powerful.

redundancy_check.correlation_note: Fruity and bold wines may sometimes be correlated, as both are often associated with New World styles and high-intensity profiles.
redundancy_check.disentanglement_suggestion: Clarify that 'bold' refers to intensity of flavor and structure (e.g., tannin, alcohol), while 'fruity' refers to the presence and type of fruit aromas and flavors. A wine can be bold but not fruity (e.g., a tannic Nebbiolo) or fruity but not bold (e.g., a sweet Riesling).
